title: Pulmonary Embolism
subtitle: From Virchow's Triad to Risk Stratification

vignette.title: The Case
vignette.text:
A 42-year-old woman presents with acute-onset shortness of breath and right-sided pleuritic chest pain that began suddenly 3 hours ago. The pain is sharp, worse with deep inspiration. She also reports feeling lightheaded and anxious.

She returned from a business trip to Tokyo 5 days ago (14-hour flight). During the flight, she remained seated most of the time due to turbulence.

PMH: Obesity (BMI 34), PCOS
Medications: Combined OCPs (ethinyl estradiol/norgestrel) for 3 years
FHx: Mother had DVT at age 50 after hip surgery
Social: Non-smoker, marketing executive

physicalExam.general: Alert, mildly anxious, speaking in short sentences
physicalExam.cardiovascular: Tachycardic, regular. Normal S1, loud P2. JVP 10 cm H2O
physicalExam.pulmonary: Tachypneic. Clear. Diminished breath sounds at right base
physicalExam.extremities: Right calf 2.5 cm larger than left with mild tenderness

ecg.description: Sinus tach 112. S1Q3T3. Right axis. Incomplete RBBB

imaging.ctpa: Filling defects in R main PA. RV:LV ratio 1.3
imaging.doppler: DVT in R popliteal extending to femoral vein

questions.stem: CT-PA shows filling defects in the right main pulmonary artery.
questions.question: The pulmonary arteries carry deoxygenated blood from which cardiac chamber?
questions.hint: Trace the path: systemic veins → right atrium → ? → pulmonary arteries → lungs
questions.explanation: The pulmonary arteries arise from the pulmonary trunk, which exits the right ventricle. When PE obstructs these arteries, it creates backpressure on the RV.

whyChain.why: Why does PE cause RV strain?
whyChain.answer: Clot obstruction increases pulmonary vascular resistance. The RV must pump against higher afterload, causing dilation (RV:LV >1.0).

whyChain.why: Why is the RV vulnerable to acute pressure overload?
whyChain.answer: The RV is thin-walled (3-5mm vs LV 10-15mm), designed for low-pressure work. It cannot acutely adapt to high afterload.

questions.stem: Doppler reveals DVT in the right popliteal vein extending to the femoral vein.
questions.question: Which venous pathway allows a DVT from the leg to reach the pulmonary arteries?
questions.hint: Trace: leg veins → femoral → iliac → ? → right heart → pulmonary arteries

options.text: Inferior vena cava → right atrium → right ventricle → pulmonary trunk
Superior vena cava → right atrium → right ventricle
Portal vein → liver → hepatic vein → right atrium
Azygos vein → superior vena cava → left atrium

questions.explanation: Lower extremity DVTs travel via: femoral → external iliac → common iliac → IVC → right atrium → RV → pulmonary trunk → pulmonary arteries.

whyChain.why: Why do most PEs originate from proximal leg DVTs?
whyChain.answer: Proximal DVTs (popliteal and above) are larger and more likely to embolize. About 50% of untreated proximal DVTs lead to PE.

questions.stem: Physical exam reveals a loud P2 (pulmonic component of S2).
questions.question: The pulmonic valve is located between which structures?
questions.hint: The pulmonic valve guards the RV outflow tract.
questions.explanation: The pulmonic valve sits at the RV outflow tract-pulmonary trunk junction. When pulmonary pressures rise, the valve closes with greater force, creating a loud P2.

whyChain.why: Why does increased pulmonary pressure cause a louder P2?
whyChain.answer: Higher pressure means the valve leaflets close against greater pressure, producing a more forceful closure sound.

questions.stem: The right calf is 2.5 cm larger than the left.
questions.question: The deep veins of the calf include all EXCEPT:
questions.hint: Deep veins run alongside arteries. The great saphenous is superficial.
questions.explanation: The great saphenous vein is SUPERFICIAL (medial leg). Deep calf veins include anterior tibial, posterior tibial, and peroneal veins. DVT typically occurs in deep veins.

whyChain.why: Why is the deep vs superficial distinction important?
whyChain.answer: Superficial thrombophlebitis rarely causes PE because superficial veins drain into deep veins at specific junctions. DVT has direct access to central circulation.

questions.stem: ABG shows pH 7.48, pCO2 28, pO2 62.
questions.question: What is the PRIMARY mechanism causing hypoxemia in acute PE?
questions.hint: Think about blood flow to unventilated lung areas (or vice versa).
questions.explanation: PE causes V/Q mismatch: (1) Dead space—occluded vessels have ventilation but no perfusion. (2) Shunt—blood diverts to non-occluded areas, overwhelming those regions.

whyChain.why: Why is pCO2 LOW (28) rather than high?
whyChain.answer: Hypoxemia triggers hyperventilation. CO2 diffuses 20x more easily than O2, so tachypnea effectively blows off CO2 (respiratory alkalosis).

questions.stem: Her heart rate is 112 bpm with mild respiratory distress.
questions.question: What is the PRIMARY stimulus driving her tachycardia?
questions.hint: When O2 drops, what receptors detect this?

options.text: Central chemoreceptor response to elevated CO2
Direct cardiac irritation from the clot

questions.explanation: Hypoxemia (PaO2 62) stimulates peripheral chemoreceptors in carotid/aortic bodies, triggering sympathetic activation → tachycardia and tachypnea.

whyChain.why: Is her tachycardia helpful or harmful?
whyChain.answer: Initially helpful (increases CO to maintain O2 delivery), but sustained tachycardia increases myocardial O2 demand while the strained RV has compromised perfusion.

questions.stem: JVP is elevated at 10 cm H2O and RV:LV ratio is 1.3 on CT.
questions.question: What physiologic principle explains why increased PVR causes RV failure?
questions.hint: What happens to a thin-walled chamber if afterload suddenly increases?

options.text: The thin-walled RV cannot generate sufficient pressure against increased afterload
Pulmonary venous congestion backs up into the right heart
Coronary spasm reduces RV blood supply

questions.explanation: The RV is designed for low-pressure work. When PE increases PVR, the thin-walled RV cannot generate enough pressure. It dilates, fails, and backs up into systemic venous circulation (elevated JVP).

whyChain.why: How does RV failure lead to systemic hypotension?
whyChain.answer: RV dilation causes: (1) decreased RV output → decreased LV filling, (2) septal shift compresses LV, (3) decreased coronary perfusion. All reduce systemic CO.

questions.stem: Her A-a gradient is elevated at 44 mmHg (normal <15).
questions.question: An elevated A-a gradient indicates:
questions.hint: A-a gradient measures the alveolar-arterial O2 difference.

options.text: Hypoxemia due to V/Q mismatch, shunt, or diffusion impairment
Hypoxemia due to decreased inspired oxygen

questions.explanation: An elevated A-a gradient means the lungs aren't efficiently transferring O2 from alveoli to blood. This occurs with V/Q mismatch, shunt, or diffusion problems. Pure hypoventilation has normal A-a gradient.

whyChain.why: Why does hypoventilation have normal A-a gradient?
whyChain.answer: In hypoventilation, both alveolar AND arterial O2 drop proportionally. The gradient stays normal because lung gas exchange is intact.

questions.stem: Multiple risk factors: recent flight, OCPs, obesity, PCOS, family history.
questions.question: Which Virchow's triad component do OCPs primarily affect?
questions.hint: Estrogen affects hepatic production of clotting factors.
questions.explanation: Estrogen increases hepatic synthesis of procoagulant factors (fibrinogen, II, VII, VIII, X) while decreasing anticoagulants (antithrombin, protein S). OCP use increases VTE risk 3-4 fold.

whyChain.why: How do her other risk factors contribute?
whyChain.answer: Flight = stasis; Obesity = stasis + inflammation + increased estrogen; PCOS = metabolic syndrome. Multiple factors are multiplicative, not additive.

questions.stem: She remained seated for most of a 14-hour flight.
questions.question: Prolonged immobility primarily contributes to VTE through:
questions.hint: When sitting for long periods, what happens to leg blood flow?

options.text: Tissue factor activation from muscle damage

questions.explanation: The calf muscles act as a 'venous pump.' Prolonged sitting eliminates this, causing blood to pool in leg veins. Stasis allows activated clotting factors to accumulate locally.

whyChain.why: Why do clots start in venous valve pockets?
whyChain.answer: Valve pockets are natural stasis areas where blood eddies form. This creates a hypoxic microenvironment where endothelial cells become prothrombotic.

questions.stem: D-dimer is markedly elevated at 4,850 ng/mL.
questions.question: D-dimer is a degradation product of:
questions.hint: D-dimer comes from fibrin that has been cross-linked.
questions.explanation: D-dimer forms when plasmin cleaves cross-linked fibrin. It indicates both clot formation AND fibrinolysis. High sensitivity (~95%) but low specificity for VTE.

whyChain.why: Why is D-dimer used to RULE OUT rather than rule in?
whyChain.answer: High sensitivity (95-97%) means a negative test reliably excludes PE in low-risk patients. Low specificity (40-50%) means positive results need imaging confirmation.

questions.stem: Her mother had DVT at age 50 after hip surgery.
questions.question: Which inherited thrombophilia is MOST common in Caucasians?
questions.hint: This mutation affects factor V, making it resistant to protein C.
questions.explanation: Factor V Leiden is present in 5% of Caucasians. It causes activated protein C resistance. Heterozygotes have 5-10x increased VTE risk.

whyChain.why: Should she be tested for thrombophilia?
whyChain.answer: Acute testing is unreliable. Testing may be considered 3+ months after treatment. However, results rarely change management.

questions.stem: Troponin I is elevated at 0.18 ng/mL.
questions.question: What is the mechanism of troponin elevation in acute PE?
questions.hint: Troponin indicates myocardial injury. What stress is the RV under?

options.text: RV strain and microinfarction from increased wall stress

questions.explanation: PE increases RV afterload. The dilated RV has increased wall tension (Laplace) and O2 demand, while coronary perfusion may be compromised. This supply-demand mismatch causes RV microinfarction.

whyChain.why: What does troponin elevation mean for prognosis?
whyChain.answer: Elevated troponin predicts worse outcomes. Combined with RV dilation, it identifies 'intermediate-high risk' patients who may need more aggressive therapy.

questions.stem: Initial anticoagulation is ordered for this intermediate-high risk PE.
questions.question: Which anticoagulant should be initiated FIRST?
questions.hint: Think about onset of action. Which class works immediately?
questions.explanation: Parenteral anticoagulation provides immediate effect. Warfarin takes 5-7 days and initially creates a prothrombotic state. DOACs require lead-in dosing.

whyChain.why: When would you choose UFH over LMWH?
whyChain.answer: UFH is preferred when: (1) thrombolysis may be needed (short half-life, reversible), (2) renal failure, (3) extreme obesity, (4) hemodynamic instability.

questions.stem: She receives UFH with target aPTT 60-80 seconds.
questions.question: UFH exerts its anticoagulant effect primarily by:
questions.hint: Heparin works through an intermediary natural anticoagulant.
questions.explanation: Heparin binds to antithrombin III, increasing AT's ability to inhibit thrombin and factor Xa by 1000-fold.

whyChain.why: Why monitor UFH with aPTT but not LMWH?
whyChain.answer: UFH has unpredictable pharmacokinetics due to variable protein binding. LMWH has more predictable absorption and response.

questions.stem: The team discusses whether thrombolysis is indicated.
questions.question: Systemic thrombolysis (alteplase) works by:
questions.hint: The body has a natural clot-dissolving system.

options.text: Converting plasminogen to plasmin, which degrades fibrin

questions.explanation: Alteplase (tPA) converts plasminogen to plasmin, which degrades fibrin. Pharmacologic doses achieve faster lysis than endogenous fibrinolysis.

whyChain.why: What are the indications for thrombolysis in PE?
whyChain.answer: Primary: Massive PE with hemodynamic instability. Controversial: Submassive PE with RV dysfunction—consider if deteriorating.

questions.stem: Plans are made for long-term anticoagulation.
questions.question: For this provoked PE (flight + OCPs), recommended anticoagulation duration is:
questions.hint: Provoked VTEs have different recurrence rates than unprovoked.
questions.explanation: For PE provoked by major transient risk factors, 3 months is standard. After removing provoking factors (discontinue OCPs), recurrence risk is ~3%/year.

whyChain.why: Should she continue OCPs after treatment?
whyChain.answer: No! Estrogen-containing contraception is contraindicated after VTE. She should use progestin-only methods, copper IUD, or barrier methods.

questions.stem: Before imaging, you need to assess pre-test probability.
questions.question: Based on Wells criteria, how should this patient be classified?
questions.hint: Wells includes: DVT signs, HR, immobilization, previous VTE, hemoptysis, cancer, clinical gestalt.

options.text: PE Likely (Wells >4): DVT signs (3) + HR>100 (1.5) + immobilization (1.5) = 6 pts

questions.explanation: Wells score = 6 points (>4 = 'PE likely'). This warrants CT-PA without D-dimer. A negative D-dimer in high-probability patients does NOT rule out PE.

whyChain.why: Why not get D-dimer on everyone?
whyChain.answer: In high pre-test probability, negative D-dimer still leaves ~15-20% PE chance. D-dimer only rules out PE in LOW probability patients.

questions.stem: CT-PA confirms PE. She's stable but has RV dilation and elevated biomarkers.
questions.question: How should she be risk-stratified according to ESC guidelines?
questions.hint: ESC stratification depends on hemodynamics, RV dysfunction, AND biomarkers.

options.text: Intermediate-high risk (stable + RV dysfunction + elevated biomarkers)

questions.explanation: ESC: High-risk = hemodynamic instability. Intermediate-high = stable + RV dysfunction + elevated biomarkers (BOTH). She has BOTH, making her intermediate-high risk.

whyChain.why: Why does intermediate-high risk matter?
whyChain.answer: ~5% will deteriorate. These patients need ICU monitoring with rescue thrombolysis ready.

questions.stem: You're considering differential diagnoses.
questions.question: Which finding BEST distinguishes PE from acute coronary syndrome?
questions.hint: Both cause chest pain, tachycardia, and elevated troponin. What's different about the pain?

options.text: Pleuritic chest pain (worse with inspiration) favors PE

questions.explanation: Pleuritic pain (sharp, worse with breathing) suggests pleural involvement—classic for PE. ACS causes pressure pain unrelated to respiration.

whyChain.why: Why is PE pain pleuritic?
whyChain.answer: Peripheral emboli cause pulmonary infarction near the pleural surface. Inflamed visceral pleura irritates parietal pleura, causing sharp, respiratory-variant pain.

questions.stem: ECG shows S1Q3T3 and incomplete RBBB.
questions.question: What is the sensitivity of S1Q3T3 for PE?
questions.hint: This classic finding is often taught but think about how often it appears.

options.text: Low (10-20%); most PEs have non-specific findings or sinus tach only

questions.explanation: S1Q3T3 is present in only 10-20% of PE. The most common ECG finding is sinus tachycardia. A normal ECG does NOT rule out PE.

whyChain.why: What does S1Q3T3 represent anatomically?
whyChain.answer: Acute RV dilation rotates the heart clockwise. S1 = increased rightward forces in lead I. Q3T3 = rightward axis shift in lead III.

questions.stem: Six hours after admission, her BP drops to 84/52 despite IV fluids.
questions.question: What is the MOST likely cause of deterioration?
questions.hint: She was intermediate-high risk. What happens as RV function declines?

options.text: Progressive RV failure with cardiogenic shock

questions.explanation: Intermediate-high risk patients can deteriorate. The RV fails progressively: decreased output → LV underfilling → hypotension → coronary hypoperfusion → worsening RV ischemia ('death spiral').

whyChain.why: What should be done now?
whyChain.answer: She now meets criteria for high-risk (massive) PE. Primary reperfusion therapy (thrombolysis, catheter-directed therapy, or surgical embolectomy) is indicated.

questions.stem: Patient asks about long-term consequences of PE.
questions.question: What is chronic thromboembolic pulmonary hypertension (CTEPH)?
questions.hint: Some clots don't fully resolve. What if they become organized fibrous tissue?

options.text: Persistent pulmonary hypertension from organized thrombi that didn't resolve
Heart failure from LV dysfunction post-PE

questions.explanation: CTEPH occurs in 2-4% of PE survivors when thrombi organize into fibrous obstructions rather than dissolving. Treatment includes pulmonary endarterectomy.

whyChain.why: How do you screen for CTEPH?
whyChain.answer: Follow up at 3-6 months. Persistent dyspnea should prompt echo and V/Q scan (more sensitive than CT for chronic disease).

questions.stem: Her platelet count drops from 268,000 to 95,000 on day 7 of heparin.
questions.question: What is the 4T score used to assess?
questions.hint: Thrombocytopenia + Timing + Thrombosis + oTher causes.
questions.explanation: The 4T score assesses HIT probability. If intermediate/high score, stop heparin and switch to non-heparin anticoagulant.

whyChain.why: Why is HIT prothrombotic despite low platelets?
whyChain.answer: HIT antibodies ACTIVATE platelets, causing aggregation and thrombin generation. Platelets are consumed in clots. It's a hypercoagulable state, not bleeding.

questions.stem: Reflecting on the case from presentation to management.
questions.question: Which combination was MOST critical for intermediate-high risk classification?
questions.hint: What separates intermediate-high from intermediate-low in ESC framework?

options.text: RV:LV ratio >1 + elevated troponin + elevated BNP
S1Q3T3 on ECG + Westermark sign on CXR

questions.explanation: ESC intermediate-high requires: (1) Hemodynamically stable, (2) RV dysfunction on imaging (RV:LV >1), AND (3) Elevated biomarkers. This identifies patients at ~5% deterioration risk.

whyChain.why: What's the key lesson about risk stratification?
whyChain.answer: Diagnosis is step 1. Risk stratification determines management: High = immediate reperfusion; Intermediate-high = ICU + rescue ready; Intermediate-low = ward; Low = consider outpatient.

questions.stem: Connecting pathophysiology to clinical presentation.
questions.question: Which cascade BEST explains the connection between her risk factors and current status?
questions.hint: Map each risk factor to Virchow's triad, then trace to RV strain.

options.text: Stasis (flight) + hypercoagulability (OCPs) → DVT → embolization → increased PVR → RV strain
Endothelial injury (OCPs) → platelet activation → DVT → hypoxemia → tachycardia
Stasis (obesity) → local hypoxia → tissue factor release → left heart failure

questions.explanation: Correct cascade: Immobility = stasis, OCPs = hypercoagulability → DVT → embolization through IVC → mechanical obstruction increases PVR → thin-walled RV struggles → dilation and strain.

whyChain.why: What's the most important intervention to prevent recurrence?
whyChain.answer: Stop estrogen-containing contraception. With VTE history, estrogen is absolutely contraindicated. This removes a major hypercoagulable risk factor.

questions.stem: Counseling the patient before discharge.
questions.question: Which statement about her prognosis is MOST accurate?
questions.hint: Consider provoked vs unprovoked VTE recurrence rates.

options.text: After 3 months of anticoagulation with OCP discontinuation, annual recurrence risk is ~3%
She requires lifelong anticoagulation given PE severity
Recurrence risk is the same whether or not she stops OCPs
Once anticoagulation stops, she has no increased risk vs general population

questions.explanation: Her PE was provoked by transient factors. After 3 months of anticoagulation and removing provoking factors, annual recurrence is ~3%—lower than unprovoked (~8%) but still elevated.

whyChain.why: What follow-up does she need?
whyChain.answer: 3-6 month follow-up for CTEPH screening if dyspnea persists, contraception counseling, consider thrombophilia testing, lifestyle modifications.
